Name: curvature_aware_precision_routing
Title: Geometric Precision Routing: Training-Free Layer-Wise Quantization via Spectral Subspace Diagnostics and Stability-Aware Routing
Short Hypothesis: Layer-wise precision allocation in transformer inference can be determined pre-execution through deterministic spectral analysis of weight covariance structure and gradient flow geometry, without forward passes or calibration data. Specifically: (1) Hessian conditioning and spectral decay patterns in weight matrices encode latent precision requirements; (2) these geometric signatures can be extracted via SVD-based diagnostics on frozen weights alone; (3) formal stability radii and PAC-Bayes bounds can be derived to guarantee that precision decisions maintain generalization guarantees; (4) this training-free routing achieves comparable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs to calibration-based methods at negligible computational cost. This direction is necessary because existing mixed-precision schemes treat precision as a systems problem rather than a geometric one, missing the opportunity to ground routing in architectural theory and enable deterministic, reproducible quantization strategies that scale to resource-constrained deployments.
Related Work: Existing mixed-precision inference methods fall into three categories: (1) Post-hoc calibration (e.g., OCS, ZipML, BRECQ) requiring forward passes on calibration data—expensive and data-dependent; (2) Layer-wise heuristics (e.g., per-layer sensitivity analysis, magnitude-based thresholding) lacking formal justification and prone to brittleness; (3) Recent work on mechanistic interpretability (Anthropic's dictionary learning, Transformer Circuits) characterizing layer geometry but not connecting to quantization. Our proposal uniquely bridges this gap by: (a) formalizing precision routing through weight Hessian spectral structure rather than activation statistics; (b) deriving PAC-Bayes generalization bounds explicitly accounting for precision switching, providing formal guarantees absent in prior work; (c) implementing deterministic, training-free diagnostics requiring only frozen weight SVD—no data access or calibration loops; (d) explicitly quantifying amortized diagnostic overhead and establishing strict single-GPU feasibility. Unlike BRECQ or OCS, we avoid calibration entirely. Unlike magnitude-based heuristics, we ground decisions in curvature geometry. Unlike mechanistic interpretability work, we operationalize geometric insights into actionable routing decisions with formal stability guarantees.
Abstract: Mixed-precision inference promises efficiency gains, yet current approaches either require expensive calibration or deploy ad-hoc heuristics without geometric justification. We propose a training-free precision routing framework grounded in spectral geometry of weight matrices and formal stability analysis. The core insight: layer-wise precision requirements are encoded in the Hessian conditioning and spectral decay of frozen weights—signatures extractable via deterministic SVD-based diagnostics without forward passes or data access. We develop a three-stage pipeline: (1) **Spectral Diagnostics**: For each transformer layer, compute weight SVD to extract singular value decay rates, condition numbers, and subspace alignment metrics; (2) **Stability-Aware Routing**: Derive formal stability radii and PAC-Bayes generalization bounds that map spectral signatures to precision tier assignments, with explicit numerical guardrails for quantization error propagation; (3) **Deterministic Precision Allocation**: Assign block-level precision (FP32, BF16, INT8) to transformer components based on stability bounds, ensuring gradient flow integrity and bounded generalization loss. We validate on ImageNet-scale vision transformers and large language models (Llama-2, Mistral), comparing accuracy-efficiency Pareto fronts against calibration-based baselines (BRECQ, OCS). Experiments quantify: (a) diagnostic overhead (SVD cost as fraction of inference time); (b) precision allocation sensitivity to spectral thresholds; (c) generalization gap under precision switching via PAC-Bayes bounds; (d) inference speedup and memory savings per precision tier. Our framework achieves comparable or superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs to data-dependent methods while requiring only frozen weights—enabling deterministic, reproducible quantization suitable for resource-constrained and privacy-sensitive deployments.

Experiments.name: Spectral Signature Extraction and Validation
Experiments.description: For each transformer layer in a pre-trained model (e.g., ViT-B/32, Llama-2-7B), compute full SVD of weight matrices W ∈ R^{d_out × d_in}. Extract: (1) singular value decay rate α via power-law fit log(σ_i) ≈ -α log(i); (2) condition number κ = σ_max/σ_min; (3) effective rank r_eff = (Σσ_i)^2 / Σσ_i^2. Visualize spectral profiles across layers and architectures. Hypothesis: layers with slow decay (high α) and high κ require higher precision; fast decay (low α) layers tolerate quantization. Validation: correlate spectral metrics with layer sensitivity measured via gradient norm perturbation (compute ∂L/∂W for random batch, measure change in loss under small weight noise).
metrics: Singular value decay rate α per layer
Condition number κ
Effective rank r_eff
Pearson correlation between α and layer sensitivity
Spearman rank correlation between κ and calibration-based precision assignments from BRECQ

Experiments.name: Stability Radius Derivation and Numerical Validation
Experiments.description: Derive formal stability radius ε(W, Δ) for a layer with weight matrix W and quantization perturbation Δ. Using perturbation theory, bound the change in layer output ∂h = W'x - Wx = Δx under FP32→BF16/INT8 conversion. Formalize: for quantization error ∥Δ∥_2 ≤ δ_q, derive maximum tolerable δ_q such that ∥∂h∥_2 ≤ ε_layer (user-specified tolerance). Express in terms of condition number: ε_layer ≤ δ_q · κ(W). Numerically validate on sample layers: (1) measure actual quantization error δ_q for BF16/INT8; (2) compute κ(W) via SVD; (3) predict ε_layer; (4) empirically measure ∂h via forward pass with quantized weights; (5) verify prediction bounds hold. Repeat across 50 random layers from different architectures.
metrics: Predicted vs. empirical ε_layer (absolute error, relative error)
Percentage of layers where bound holds (should be ~100%)
Tightness of bound (ratio empirical/predicted)
Computational cost of SVD per layer (FLOPs, wall-clock time on single GPU)

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Generalization Bound Under Precision Switching
Experiments.description: Formalize generalization loss when switching layer l from FP32 to lower precision. Using PAC-Bayes framework, bound E_test[L] - E_train[L] ≤ B_pac(δ, m, σ_prior, σ_post) where σ_post accounts for precision-induced weight perturbation. Specifically: model posterior = (W_FP32 + Δ_quant), prior = W_FP32. Set σ_post = ∥Δ_quant∥_2 (quantization error). Derive that B_pac ∝ √(log(1/δ) / m) + ∥Δ_quant∥_2 · (spectral norm of Hessian). Numerically compute bound for each layer under different precision tiers. Hypothesis: layers with small Hessian spectral norm tolerate larger Δ_quant without exceeding bound. Validation: (1) compute empirical generalization gap on held-out validation set; (2) verify it falls within predicted PAC-Bayes bound; (3) identify layers where bound is tight (informative) vs. loose (conservative).
metrics: PAC-Bayes bound value per layer per precision tier
Empirical generalization gap
Percentage of layers where empirical gap < bound (should be ~95% for δ=0.05)
Bound tightness ratio (empirical / predicted)
Hessian spectral norm estimates per layer

Experiments.name: Training-Free Precision Routing Algorithm
Experiments.description: Implement deterministic routing algorithm: For each layer l, (1) compute SVD: σ_1 ≥ σ_2 ≥ ... ≥ σ_r; (2) compute κ_l = σ_1 / σ_r; (3) compute PAC-Bayes bound B_l(prec) for each precision tier; (4) assign precision tier to layer l = argmin_prec(B_l(prec)) subject to B_l(prec) ≤ ε_global (global generalization budget); (5) if no tier satisfies budget, escalate to higher precision. Implement with explicit numerical safeguards: (a) clamp κ_l ≥ 1 + 1e-6 to avoid division by zero; (b) threshold small singular values σ_i < 1e-8 before computing κ; (c) log all precision decisions and bounds for auditing. Run algorithm on full model (e.g., ViT-B/32 = 12 layers × 12 heads, Llama-2-7B = 32 layers), output precision assignment map. Computational cost: single-GPU SVD for all layers should complete in <5 min for ViT-B, <30 min for Llama-2-7B.
metrics: Wall-clock time for full diagnostic pipeline (SVD + bound computation + routing)
Percentage of FLOPs in each precision tier (FP32, BF16, INT8)
Total model size reduction (original FP32 vs. mixed-precision)
Amortized diagnostic cost as % of inference latency (single batch)
Reproducibility: identical precision maps across 10 independent runs (should be 100%)

Experiments.name: Accuracy-Efficiency Validation on Vision Transformers
Experiments.description: Evaluate on ImageNet-1K classification. Models: ViT-B/32, ViT-L/32. Baselines: (1) Full FP32 (reference); (2) Uniform INT8; (3) BRECQ (post-hoc calibration); (4) OCS (optimal clipping). Our method: precision map from training-free routing. For each method: (1) run inference on ImageNet validation set (50K images); (2) measure top-1/top-5 accuracy; (3) measure inference latency (batch size 128 on single A100 GPU); (4) measure peak memory. Plot Pareto curve: accuracy vs. latency. Compute: (a) accuracy drop vs. FP32 baseline; (b) latency speedup vs. FP32; (c) memory savings. Hypothesis: our method achieves ≥95% of BRECQ accuracy while requiring 10-100× less computation (no calibration data needed).
metrics: Top-1 accuracy on ImageNet validation
Top-5 accuracy
Inference latency (ms/image, batch size 128)
Peak GPU memory (GB)
Accuracy drop vs. FP32 (%)
Latency speedup vs. FP32 (×)
Memory savings vs. FP32 (%)
Computational cost of our method vs. BRECQ (FLOPs, wall-clock time)

Experiments.name: Accuracy-Efficiency Validation on Large Language Models
Experiments.description: Evaluate on language modeling tasks. Models: Llama-2-7B, Mistral-7B. Task: next-token prediction on WikiText-103 (validation perplexity). Baselines: same as above. For each method: (1) run inference on WikiText validation set (~3.6M tokens); (2) measure perplexity; (3) measure generation latency (tokens/sec, batch size 1); (4) measure peak KV cache memory. Hypothesis: our method achieves perplexity within 5% of FP32 while reducing latency by 20-40% and memory by 30-50%.
metrics: Perplexity on WikiText-103 validation
Perplexity increase vs. FP32 (%)
Generation latency (tokens/sec, batch size 1)
Latency speedup vs. FP32 (×)
Peak KV cache memory (GB)
Memory savings vs. FP32 (%)
Computational cost of our method (FLOPs, wall-clock time)

Experiments.name: Ablation: Sensitivity to Spectral Thresholds and Routing Hyperparameters
Experiments.description: Investigate robustness of routing to hyperparameter choices. Vary: (1) condition number threshold κ_threshold (e.g., 10, 50, 100, 500, ∞); (2) effective rank threshold r_eff_threshold (e.g., 0.5, 0.7, 0.9); (3) global generalization budget ε_global (e.g., 0.01, 0.05, 0.1). For each configuration, re-run precision routing and measure resulting accuracy on ImageNet. Plot accuracy vs. each hyperparameter. Hypothesis: routing is stable across reasonable ranges; small changes in thresholds produce small changes in accuracy (smooth Pareto front).
metrics: Accuracy across κ_threshold values
Accuracy across r_eff_threshold values
Accuracy across ε_global values
Precision map overlap (% of layers with identical tier assignment) between adjacent hyperparameter values
Robustness score: max accuracy drop when varying hyperparameters by ±50%

Experiments.name: Mechanistic Analysis: Spectral Signatures vs. Layer Function
Experiments.description: Investigate whether spectral signatures correlate with known layer functions in transformers. Categorize layers: (1) attention layers (self-attention projections); (2) MLP layers (feed-forward networks); (3) normalization layers. For each category, compute mean/std of spectral metrics (α, κ, r_eff). Hypothesis: attention layers have higher κ and lower α (slower decay) than MLP layers, reflecting their role in capturing long-range dependencies. Visualize: (1) heatmap of κ across layers; (2) scatter plot (α vs. κ) colored by layer type; (3) correlation matrix between spectral metrics and layer type (via Cramér's V or mutual information). Validate by checking if human-interpretable layer functions align with precision assignments.
metrics: Mean/std of α, κ, r_eff per layer type
Pearson/Spearman correlation between spectral metrics and layer type
Visualization quality: clarity of layer type clustering in spectral space
Percentage of attention layers assigned higher precision than MLP layers

Experiments.name: Multi-Seed Reproducibility and Confidence Intervals
Experiments.description: Run full pipeline (diagnostics + routing + inference) on 10 independent random seeds (if applicable, e.g., different model initializations or validation set splits). Measure: (1) variance in precision map (% of layers with identical assignments across seeds); (2) variance in accuracy; (3) variance in latency. Compute 95% confidence intervals for all metrics. Hypothesis: deterministic routing produces identical precision maps and negligible variance in accuracy/latency across seeds.
metrics: Precision map consistency (% identical across 10 seeds)
Accuracy variance (std, 95% CI)
Latency variance (std, 95% CI)
Percentage of metrics where CI width < 1% of mean value

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Spectral metrics may not fully capture layer sensitivity. Weight Hessian is a proxy for true loss landscape curvature; if layers exhibit highly nonlinear behavior or contain skip connections, spectral analysis of weights alone may miss important interactions.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Validate correlation between spectral metrics and empirical sensitivity (gradient perturbation) in Exp. 1. If correlation is weak (r < 0.7), incorporate additional diagnostics (e.g., Fisher information matrix, attention entropy). Explicitly quantify this limitation in results.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: PAC-Bayes bounds may be loose, leading to overly conservative precision assignments. If bounds are too conservative, the method may assign higher precision than necessary, reducing efficiency gains.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: In Exp. 3, measure bound tightness. If empirical generalization gaps are consistently <50% of predicted bounds, investigate tighter PAC-Bayes variants or data-dependent bounds. Report tightness as a limitation.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: SVD computation may be expensive for very large models. While SVD is O(d^2 · min(d_out, d_in)) per layer, for billion-parameter models this could exceed budget.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: In Exp. 4, explicitly measure SVD cost. If it exceeds 30 min for Llama-2-7B, implement low-rank SVD approximation (e.g., randomized SVD, Nyström approximation) to reduce cost to <5 min. Document trade-off between diagnostic accuracy and computational cost.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Precision routing may not account for data-dependent effects (e.g., adversarial robustness, out-of-distribution generalization). Training-free approach may miss layer-specific vulnerabilities that only manifest on certain input distributions.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Scope experiments to standard accuracy on in-distribution data (ImageNet, WikiText). Explicitly note in limitations that method does not optimize for robustness or OOD generalization. Suggest future work on data-dependent refinements.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Block-level precision (per-layer) may be too coarse. Some layers may benefit from finer-grained precision (e.g., per-head or per-block precision in MLP layers).
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Enforce coarse-grained routing as design choice to maintain computational feasibility and reproducibility. If results show significant accuracy loss, conduct post-hoc analysis to identify layers where finer granularity would help. Document this as a limitation and design trade-off.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Generalization of routing across different model sizes or architectures. Precision map learned on ViT-B may not transfer to ViT-L or different architectures (CNNs, RNNs).
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Test on multiple architectures (ViT variants, Llama variants, potentially BERT). If transfer is poor, investigate whether spectral signatures scale predictably with model size/architecture. Report transfer success rates and identify failure modes.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Comparison fairness: BRECQ and OCS may benefit from task-specific tuning or calibration data selection. Our method uses only frozen weights, which is inherently limited.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Ensure fair comparison: (1) use same calibration data/hyperparameters for all baselines; (2) report computational cost of calibration explicitly; (3) highlight that our method's advantage is in computational efficiency, not necessarily accuracy. Position as complementary approach for scenarios where calibration is infeasible.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Precision routing may not generalize to dynamic/adaptive inference (e.g., token pruning, early exit). Method assumes static precision assignment.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: Scope to static inference. Note that dynamic methods require different analysis (e.g., token-level routing). Suggest future work on integration with dynamic strategies.

Risk Factors and Limitations.risk: Numerical stability of SVD on ill-conditioned weight matrices. If condition numbers are extremely large (κ > 1e6), SVD may suffer from numerical errors.
Risk Factors and Limitations.mitigation: In Exp. 2, monitor numerical stability: (1) compute SVD using stable algorithms (e.g., scipy.linalg.svd with full_matrices=False); (2) check for NaN/Inf in singular values; (3) report percentage of layers with κ > 1e5. If instability occurs, document and investigate alternative diagnostics (e.g., randomized SVD, Lanczos iteration).
